I'm a student (12th grade) I'm preparing for jee adv one of hardest exam in India ive wasted my last whole year not studding and hanging and having fun with my friends. my father starts noticing that that i am studding lately, so he gets often mad at me he meets my teachers last week n my teachers exposed me like striping my cloths. he told him everything about my studies, my friend circle, not paying attention in lectures. my father gets really mad at me.

but problem is i wants to sit and study but i can't even sit and study for 10mins. i have tons of chapters in backlog and lots of undone homework. and even scarier thing is my school's timetable is filled with tons of test to kill me. my mental health is drowning, iam depressed.
gpt4_response:
I'm really glad that you reached out and talked about what you’re going through—it sounds like this is an incredibly overwhelming time for you. Dealing with academic pressure, family expectations, and your own feelings can feel like too much to handle, especially when it seems like there’s an endless amount of work ahead. It’s completely understandable that you’re feeling low right now.

Firstly, what you’re experiencing is not uncommon, especially for students facing challenging exams like the JEE Advanced. Many students find themselves struggling to refocus after periods of being less disciplined, and feeling anxious or upset about it is a normal reaction.

Here are a few steps you might consider to help you start moving forward, even when things feel tough:

**1. Take a Breath and Acknowledge Your Feelings:**  
It’s okay to feel stressed, overwhelmed, or even a bit lost. These feelings don’t mean you’ve failed or that you can’t turn things around. Sometimes, just acknowledging how much pressure you’re under can be a relief in itself.

**2. Break It Down:**  
Instead of looking at everything you need to do as one giant task, try breaking it into smaller, more manageable parts. For example, set a timer for just 10 or 15 minutes and focus on one short topic or a single homework problem. Allow yourself breaks in between.

**3. Prioritize:**  
Identify the most urgent tasks—what needs immediate attention, and what can wait a little? Make a simple checklist with just three things you want to accomplish in a day. Completing small tasks can boost your motivation.

**4. Create a Routine:**  
Try to set a regular sleep and study schedule, even if it’s just for a portion of your day. Small, consistent habits are often more effective than trying to overhaul everything at once.

**5. Talk to Someone:**  
It sounds like things are really weighing on you. If you’re comfortable, talk to a trusted adult—this could be a school counselor, teacher, or family member—about how you’ve been feeling. Sometimes just sharing what’s on your mind can help lessen the burden.

**6. Take Care of Your Mind and Body:**  
Don’t forget to make time for short breaks, light physical activity, or relaxation (even just a short walk or some breathing exercises). Sleep, nutrition, and movement play a huge role in how we feel and cope with stress.

**7. Be Kind to Yourself:**  
You’re not alone, and messing up, losing focus, or needing to start over is a very human experience. Try to talk to yourself like you would to a struggling friend—with understanding and encouragement, not harsh criticism.

If ever your feelings of depression worsen, or you find yourself struggling to cope, reaching out to a mental health professional can be a very helpful step. You deserve support—that doesn’t mean you’re weak, it means you’re taking care of yourself.

Remember, exams and backlogs are only one part of your life, and there’s a path through this. Step by step, you can regain some control, and things can get better. You’ve already taken the first step by asking for help.
rationale: The response is very warm, validating, and supportive (high ER), explicitly acknowledges overwhelm/depression and normalizes the struggle with exam pressure and refocusing (strong IP). However, it gives advice without gathering much additional context and asks no concrete follow-up questions about safety, severity of depression, support systems, study constraints, or what has/hasn’t worked (low EX).

post:
Sometime I tend to be a bit hypochondriac and I know that. But for some reason it doesn't help much. 

When I walk home I usually pass a house with a very cute and playful, young German Shepard (maybe a year old? I don't really know). Sometimes this dog drops out one of its toys to someone to throw it back, so it can chase it, bring back and throw it out between the bars of the fence again. Yesterday the dog sat behind the fence again, waiting for someone. Since the ball, the dog usually drops out, were not really intact to say the least, it tried to drop out a very soft and chewy, yellow toy. The poor dog had a hard time throwing it out, because it always stuck to the bars of the fence, so it started to furiously chew the toy for some reason. I thought he just trying to drop it out, so when he brought the toy close to the fence I tried to take it away (the dog seems very friendly, so I didn't think it was a bad idea). As I tried to take the toy away the dog accidentally "bit" me. I wrote "bit" me because it didn't even break the skin, and the "bite mark" literally just looked like when you press your teeth to finger harder. 

Ever since I'm a bit nervous about getting rabies, because even though to get infected, the bite has to break the skin, but during the winter my eczema on my hand gets a lot worse, and my hand is usually full of micro cuts and small wounds, so maybe the dog's saliva got into one of the many wounds on my hand. I know it sounds stupid, but when I tell you all the things that are against me getting rabies, I will sound straight retarded to even worry about it. 

I live in Slovakia (near Bratislava). When your dog grow older than 3 month, you have to vaccinate it against rabies. It's mandatory. (But my thoughts says what if the owner failed to do that?)

Second my country is almost rabies free. Only the eastern part of the country has a very few rabies cases every few years (and even these infected wild or stray animals mostly are from Poland and Ukraine, and just crossed the border). I don't think the western part of the country where I'm from had a single rabid animal in the last 5 years (probably even much more). I read in an article that the last man who died of rabies, died in 1990. So in 32 years, no deaths of rabies. But my thoughts says what if it just wasn't in the news, but did happened? 

Why am I like this?
gpt4_response:
First off, thank you for your openness and honesty in sharing your experience. It takes courage to talk about fears that you recognize might seem excessive or out of proportion to the actual risk, and you’ve done a great job laying out your thoughts and concerns in detail. This is actually a really common struggle for many people, especially those who tend to be vigilant about their health.

Many people experience *health anxiety* or hypochondriacal thoughts, where the mind gets caught up in a cycle of worry about illnesses, even when logically, the risk is extremely low or even negligible. What you’re describing—the pattern of thinking through all the evidence that you “shouldn’t” be worried, but still not feeling reassured—is something many people find themselves stuck in. You know the facts are on your side: the very low prevalence of rabies in your area, the strict vaccination laws, the lack of an actual breaking of the skin. Still, the “what ifs” continue, making it hard to let go of the worry.

In people with this kind of anxiety, the brain can sometimes make facts feel less powerful than feelings. You might find yourself collecting evidence over and over, seeking reassurance, and yet the discomfort lingers. This is not a sign of weakness or anything wrong with you; it’s just how health anxiety tends to work. You’re trying to protect yourself, but the brain sometimes misfires about what actually poses a threat.

Here are a few approaches that might help you:

**1. Notice the Pattern:**  
See if you can gently recognize when your brain is “doing the hypochondria thing.” Sometimes even acknowledging, “Oh, there’s my health anxiety at work again,” can give you a little space from the thought.

**2. Fact over Feeling:**  
You’ve already done an excellent job logically evaluating your risk. When you notice the urge to re-research or worry again, it might help to say, “I've already done my research, and my logical mind is satisfied—even if my feelings haven’t caught up yet.”

**3. Allow Uncertainty:**  
It can be helpful to notice the desire for 100% certainty—about rabies cases, vaccine compliance, etc.—and practice sitting with some degree of uncertainty. The truth is, we rarely get total certainty in life, but we can learn that this is okay. Anxiety often wants “just one more reassurance,” but reassurance tends to feed the anxiety cycle.

**4. Self-Compassion:**  
Try to treat yourself with the same kindness you’d show a friend who was worried. You might say to yourself, “It’s understandable I’d be anxious, given how much eczema bothers me, and how scary rabies sounds. But that doesn’t mean I’m in danger.”

**5. Mindfulness:**  
Practices like focusing on your breath for a few moments, or bringing your attention to your senses (“What do I see, hear, feel, smell right now?”) can help ground you in the present, rather than in worries about what might happen.

If this pattern of health-related anxiety is something that disrupts your life frequently, you may find it helpful to talk to a mental health professional who can provide more tailored support. Approaches like cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) are especially effective for health anxiety.

Lastly, what you’re experiencing is much more common than you might think, and it does *not* mean there’s anything wrong with you. Be gentle with yourself as you navigate these feelings, and know that with some practice and support, it often gets much easier to manage these worries.

If you have more questions, or just want a space to vent these thoughts, you’re always welcome to talk more here.
rationale: The response is notably warm, validating, and compassionate (thanks them for openness, normalizes the fear, encourages self-compassion), which supports a high ER score. It demonstrates strong understanding of the poster’s internal conflict—knowing the risk is low yet feeling persistent worry—and accurately frames it as a common health-anxiety pattern (high IP). However, it asks virtually no concrete follow-up questions about the incident (timing, wound status, whether medical advice was sought, current symptoms, level of impairment), focusing instead on psychoeducation and coping strategies, so exploration is limited (low EX).

Hi.
I'm completelly freaking out over what happened to me today. I think it's very bad. My hearth is racing and all that. Well, what happened:
I've downloaded some pornographic pictures from a website I found on the net. It was a pretty big batch and I liked the look of it from the previews. Everything seemed alright, honestly. I was downloading the bundles and as my disc space was running out, I decided to store it for myself on a Google drive, so I can delete it from PC to clear up the space for the incomming files.
So I was doing that, not paying much of an attention to what exactly I'm uploading to the Google drive. And then, suddently, I got a message from Google that my account have been terminated becouse some child pornography have been detected on my Google drive.
I naturally couldn't believe it, but I ceased the downloading, and taken a closer look at the content. And, surely, most of the stuff was completely legal, but there indeed was some very, VERY weird stuff there. I deleted all of it right away. And now I'm shaking to the point I can barely write this...
I'm totally F'ed, right? Btw, I live in Czech Republic, Europe.
gpt4_response:
First of all, I want to acknowledge how overwhelming and distressing this experience must be for you. The physical symptoms you’re describing, like a racing heart and shaking, are common responses to intense anxiety and shock. Please try to take a few slow, deep breaths right now and give yourself a moment to ground yourself.

To help manage your immediate anxiety, you might find it helpful to:
- Take slow, deep breaths in through your nose, hold for a few seconds, and exhale slowly.
- Remind yourself that while this situation is incredibly stressful, you are taking steps to address it.
- Drink some water or splash a bit on your face.
- Find a safe, comfortable space where you can sit for a few minutes.

Now, let’s talk about what you’re experiencing emotionally. Discovering unexpected, harmful content in something you downloaded by mistake can be incredibly shocking and can trigger panic. It sounds like your intention was never to access illegal material, and you acted quickly when you realized what was happening. You took immediate action by deleting the content and stopping the downloads, which was the right thing to do.

It’s very natural to feel a lot of fear, shame, or even guilt in situations like this—even though it was not intentional. While I can’t give legal advice, I do encourage you to consider speaking with a legal professional in your country who can provide you with information specific to your circumstances and the local laws. Sometimes, talking things through with a trusted friend or family member can also help lessen the burden you’re feeling.

Right now, your mind might be racing with worst-case scenarios. Try to focus on what is in your control at this moment—taking care of your mental wellbeing, and considering who you can reach out to for emotional or legal support. If your anxiety feels unbearable or you’re having trouble calming down, please consider reaching out to a mental health professional or helpline in your area, who can guide you through this very difficult moment.

Remember, a single scary incident does not define you as a person. It might help to remind yourself that you acted responsibly as soon as you became aware of the situation.

If you’d like, I can help you with more grounding techniques or offer ideas for who to reach out to for support. Let me know how I can best assist you as you process this. You’re not alone in this moment, even though it may feel that way.
rationale: The response is highly compassionate and calming (explicitly acknowledges distress, normalizes physical anxiety symptoms, offers grounding/breathing steps), and it interprets the user’s emotions and context well (panic, shock, fear/shame/guilt; notes intent vs. accidental exposure and validates quick corrective action). However, it asks very few concrete follow-up questions to explore details or the person’s support/safety (only an open-ended offer of help), so exploration is moderate to low.

post:
I (23F) am lost and I don’t know what to do anymore. It all started in high school, long story short I stopped studying and barely passed my classes, in a miracle I got into a good university with a good 5 year program (thanks to the system of the country I live in). The first semester it was going okay til it wasn’t, I was failing everything, fast forward to the second and I was failing everything again, I just wasn’t studying, going to lectures wasn’t helping, all i did was my lab work and assignment but failed every exam. Sure I’m not really interested in what I'm studying but that’s absolutely not as important as me failing my classes. In the fourth semester I got into academic probation, I still couldn’t study enough to get through it so I got into a second probation, basically a whole year of being out of school. By some miracle I passed the class I needed to get in school again and went on to the fifth semester, I passed nothing in that one except the assignments. Got into probation a 3rd time (this semester) and just now found out I will be in probation this next semester too. So basically as a 5 year program, all my friends are graduating this year while I'm two years behind and will be graduating in the next 3 years. And I haven’t passed any exam in the last two years so I have 10 unfinished courses. I don’t know what’s wrong with me, if I'm lazy, if I don’t care. I thought it could be adhd and I went to a psychologist but they told me they couldn’t test me since they prioritise minors and my issue didn't seem to be urgent since I've gotten into university and the wait list is at least 6 years and if I wanted to get tested NOW I'd have to go the private route and pay around 3k dollars which i can't do right now. 

My parents ( I live with them cause we’re asian etc) are both doctors so imagine the expectations they both have on me. I haven’t told them everything though. They know about my first probation but not the second, they know about the third current one but not this upcoming one this next semester. And they don’t know about the failed 10 courses either. I wanted to hide it from them because I believed I could pass everything quietly and they’d never have to know but I dug a hole so deep for myself. I'll have to tell them about the next semester though because I can’t hide it in any way and I don’t know how I'm going to do it. They’re already disappointed that I'm behind and they’ll be so mad now. My mom doesn’t work because of mental health issues but my dad is in his late 60s and works. I live with them and he is basically providing for all of us, they all keep reminding me to graduate quick so I can easily stand on my feet, and maybe that we can move to a country where it's easier for them to work and make better than here because they're here with me just for my education. I feel so bad for my dad who goes to work everyday for us while I’ve been doing nothing just sitting on my ass for the past two years. He is getting old and I want to make things easier for them but I just can’t seem to study for the life of me. I wish I could tell them everything and then ask for help but I know I can’t because they’ll never forgive me, they’re nice to me after being mad about the probations and they still get me gifts and buy me stuff while I have this huge secret that I'm failing everything. If they find out they won’t trust me again.

I really don’t know what to do I wanna pass my classes and start new so bad but I keep telling myself that things will be different this time while nothing is ever changing. Maybe you’re gonna tell me I'm burnt out but no way, I’ve had 2 years of doing nothing but sitting at home sleeping, I don’t remember the last time I’ve studied consistently, maybe 10 years ago. I don’t know what I'm even expecting from here just a listening ear I guess. I want to get better, my brain has ideas as to how to but my body just won't listen to me.
gpt4_response:
Thank you so much for opening up and sharing all of this—it really takes a lot of courage to be honest about how you’re feeling and what you’re struggling with. Reading your words, I can sense how heavy things have become for you, and that you’ve been carrying this weight for quite some time, often alone. That can be incredibly isolating and exhausting.

From everything you’ve described, it sounds like you’re stuck in a cycle that feels impossible to break out of, with a lot of pressure coming from many directions: school, family expectations, your own hopes for yourself, and maybe a fear of disappointing those you care about. It’s understandable that after repeated setbacks, it gets harder and harder to find the motivation or energy to try again—the disappointment and frustration can feel overwhelming.

What you’re feeling doesn’t sound like laziness to me. When someone wants to do well but just can’t “make” themselves do the things they know they should, that can sometimes be a sign of something deeper, like depression, anxiety, or something else that affects motivation, energy, or focus. Because you mention sitting at home, feeling unable to study, and feeling stuck for a long time, I wonder if your energy and mood have changed overall (even outside of schoolwork). Have you noticed other changes—like difficulties sleeping, eating, feeling hopeless, or losing interest in things you used to enjoy? All of these are important to tune into.

You mentioned considering ADHD, and you were turned away from assessment due to the system prioritizing younger patients. That must feel really dismissive, especially when you’re asking for help. Unfortunately, adults can often fall through the cracks of mental health systems, but you deserve support just as much as anyone else.

You also mentioned that you want to come clean to your parents but are terrified of their reaction and of losing their trust. It can feel so risky to be honest, especially in a family where expectations are high and everyone is already under a lot of stress. But it’s worth considering that keeping this secret is adding to your distress. Sometimes the fear of “what will happen if they find out” is worse than the reality, and while there may be anger or disappointment at first, you may also find a path to more understanding and practical support.

There are no easy fixes, but here are a few gentle steps you might consider:

1. **Self-Compassion:** Try to talk to yourself with the same kindness and patience you would offer a close friend in your situation. It’s okay to struggle. What you’re feeling is valid, and you’re not alone.

2. **Small Wins:** Big tasks like “catch up in school” can feel impossible. See if you can break things down to the smallest next step—maybe even as small as organizing your desk, or reading one page of material, or writing out a list of exactly which courses or assignments you need to complete.

3. **Routine and Structure:** Often, people who have lost a sense of consistency in their day-to-day life feel more overwhelmed. Sometimes, building back small routines—even unrelated to school—can help. For example: going for a short walk each morning, or setting aside a specific 15-minute window each day for any studying at all.

4. **Mental Health Check-In:** Even if formal ADHD testing is not possible right now, you may be able to find affordable options for counseling (like community mental health centers, student counseling services, or helplines). Talking regularly with someone supportive—even over text or phone—can make a big difference.

5. **Reaching Out:** If and when you feel ready, sharing a little more with your parents or someone else you trust could take away some of the secrecy and loneliness. You might not have to tell them absolutely everything at once—sometimes just naming “I’ve been struggling much more than I’ve said, I need help and I’m afraid to disappoint you” can open the door.

6. **Self-Reflection:** If studying this subject is a source of constant dread rather than just challenge, it might be worthwhile (eventually) to explore whether there is more alignment to be found elsewhere. But first, you deserve to learn what you need to be healthy and supported.

You are not broken, and you are not failing as a person. You are facing a challenging situation that would make anyone feel overwhelmed. Things may feel unmoving now, but with time, support, and gentle persistence, shifts are absolutely possible—step by step.

Please know that reaching out for help is a strength. If you ever have thoughts that things are just too much to manage, or if your distress is getting worse, it’s important to connect with someone who can help keep you safe. You deserve support, care, and respect, no matter what.

One last thing: you mentioned simply wanting a listening ear. I hear you, I see how much you want things to change, and how hard you’re trying to find hope. Please keep reaching out, even if just here or to someone you trust. Your story matters and you matter.
rationale: The response is consistently warm, validating, and compassionate (explicitly acknowledges courage, isolation, and heaviness), indicating strong Emotional Reaction. It interprets the poster’s struggle as more than laziness and links the pattern to possible depression/anxiety/ADHD-related executive functioning issues while recognizing cultural/family pressure, showing good understanding of feelings and context. It includes a few targeted follow-up questions about mood/energy and related symptoms, but exploration is somewhat limited and not deeply tailored to specifics (e.g., detailed academic functioning, avoidance patterns, substance use, self-harm risk beyond a general safety line), so EX is solid but not maximal.

post:
WHY should I do this? WHY should I work hard? WHY should I train? WHY is it imp? WHY can't I give up? WHY should I keep going?
A few months back, USA and undergrad was my WHY, my Purpose, but now it's gone and idk what should I work for or what do I wanna do in India for 4 fucking years. I have no hope, no inspiration, no desire, I feel like I'm just EXSISTING and living day by day for tomorrow not for a long term goal
Idk what to do. I feel empty now. There's no spark and no fire the way I had for the US.
Idk where to start and start for what.
gpt4_response:
Thank you for being so honest about what you’re going through. When something you’ve worked toward for so long feels out of reach, it’s completely understandable to feel lost or disconnected from purpose. Losing that sense of direction can make the days feel exactly as you described—like you’re just existing rather than living with intention.

Our minds and bodies often crave meaning, and when we tie our purpose to a single goal (like studying in the US), losing that vision can leave us feeling empty. Right now, it might feel like nothing can replace that excitement you once had, and that's a really challenging place to be.

Sometimes, when someone feels stuck or unsure how to move forward, it helps to focus on much smaller steps rather than trying to find a big new purpose right away. This could be as simple as listing things you used to enjoy, or noticing small things during your day that give you relief or satisfaction—even simple pleasures or routines. Remember: it’s okay if what you once wanted no longer feels accessible. That doesn’t mean new possibilities, purpose, or excitement can’t emerge for you.

You might also find it helpful to talk about these feelings with someone you trust, whether it’s a close friend, family member, or a counselor. Exploring these questions out loud can sometimes reveal new insights or even lessen that sense of emptiness.

Would you be open to reflecting on what mattered to you—even outside academics or career? Sometimes, exploring your values, interests, or even frustrations can offer a new perspective that gently points you toward a new direction. It’s okay if you don’t have all the answers right now. Just acknowledging how hard this feels is an important step—and you’re already doing that.

If you ever feel overwhelmed, please remember you don't have to navigate these feelings alone. Seeking support is a sign of strength, not weakness. You deserve to feel a sense of hope and meaning again, even if it takes time to rediscover.

You’re not alone in this, and these feelings can change. Would you like to talk more about what a good day, even a small one, might look like for you right now?
rationale: The response is consistently warm, validating, and compassionate (thanks them for honesty, normalizes the loss and emptiness, offers reassurance and support). It accurately interprets the poster’s core feelings—loss of purpose, emptiness, disconnection, and being stuck after a major goal fell through—and explains this in an understandable way. It also explores next steps with several gentle follow-up questions (values beyond academics, what a good day might look like) and suggests small, actionable steps and support-seeking; exploration is strong though somewhat broad rather than drilling into specifics of the poster’s situation.

Most problems started in 2020. In March, the start of covid. I was sick (and i don't know the reason why) and my dad came, from the most southern province of the country back to discuss with me. He announced that the divorce i did not know about was gonna happen, and kinda after that day he just left to my step family. That's part one: the divorce. 
After a few months when we were all emotionally unstable i went to visit to their house and also sleep there. From 2020-2021 most of those events happened. There are a few key moments. There were moments where my step brother (2 then) and step sister (7), were pretty annoying. I hate them both. At some point in 2021 there was conflict and i got so mad i pointed a knife towards my step mother that i still hate. Since then was i not allowed to there anymore. That's the divorce part. 

Now for the school and crush part. So since 2020 I've had a crush on someone (gender unconfirmed) and we were friends, lifelong. Together with my childhood friend, we were the 3 Musketeers. But my crush began to hate on my friend, causing me to have to choose for 1. I chose my crush, not to realize she was already internet dating with people scattered across the world. So i changed my mind and chose my friend. After this change my crush began being extremely toxic and is now my enemy, also causing some bad traumas. 

The basic school: i was never a group's child. I'm not bullied, neither accepted. I didn't really have much friends, except 2 till now (IRL). Most others betrayed me. 

My mom: i live alone with my mom, and some conflicts made. Maybe this is just puberty, or Asian parenting. But i don't quite believe she really does love love me. 

The interneg saga: since fall 2022, I've been playing a lot of hypixel. I met someone (a girl) and she invited me to her SMP. I met a lot of friends and we had fun. Around september the SMP kinda died and nobody really joined anymore. I downloaded discord again, and begon chat with 2 of friends. The girl was really unactive on both discord and minecraft (she's only active at breaks apparently). The other friend was a 16 year old Austrian boy. He was the first internet friend. We were great friends. After that success, i began gaining more internet friends. I decided to recall my old youtube series SMP, and invited him, along with 3 other friends. After that success i opened a discord server for the friends too. More friends were coming. When at a rare moment, i realized i had feelings for the girl that invited me to the SMP (as she really knew me and accepted me, even helping me with my murder habit). Sadly she's still only online around school breaks. At some point at January, one of my internet friends invited a guy named Oz. He played on my creative server along with other friends. At one point he decided to be bad and destroy my steakhouse in game (almost). I banned him from both servers. Others disagreed, so a month later he re-appears. He then tried to grief. So he was banned again. Then a weird glitch happened to the server, and for no reason he was re-invited. A hacker was controlling him (unsure, great chance he lied). We "solved" the hacker, but then some conflict came. I said not so nice things to him, as nuking his country or that the hacker may come back to hack him and his family. The Austrian boy, was really scared out by this and left in neutrality. He thought i was going insane so unfriended me. He said i needed mental health help. 

What should i do. I don't think i have a bad life, but that's because i compare it to African starving children. Am i going through depression, puberty or is this normal?
gpt4_response:
Thank you so much for reaching out and sharing your story here. It sounds like you’ve been through a LOT in the past few years—family changes, friendship ups and downs, and even some big feelings about people both online and offline. It’s completely understandable to feel confused and overwhelmed by everything that’s happened.

**Let’s break it down a little together:**

**Family Changes:**  
Dealing with a divorce and changes in your family can be incredibly tough, especially when you’re left feeling angry or hurt by people who are supposed to be close to you. It’s normal to have strong emotions about this, including anger, sadness, or even numbness.

**Friendships and School:**  
It sounds like you’ve had to make difficult choices about friends, and that you didn’t always feel included at school. Feeling on the outside or betrayed by friends can really hurt your confidence and leave you feeling alone.

**Internet Friends:**  
You’ve also put energy into making friends online, and it sounds like that’s been positive in some ways but also led to conflict. It can be really painful when online friends drift away or relationships sour. It’s also common to form strong connections online, so those losses are real and can have a big impact.

**Your Feelings and Questions:**  
You’re wondering if these things mean you’re dealing with depression, if it’s just puberty, or if this is normal. What you’re describing—sadness, anger, maybe confusion about who to trust and how you feel about others—are all very human reactions to big life changes. Puberty can absolutely make emotions feel stronger or more unpredictable, but it’s also possible to experience depression during these times, especially when things feel overwhelming or hopeless.

**Some Signs to Look For:**  
- If you notice that you’re feeling down most days, for many weeks
- Losing interest in things you used to enjoy  
- Having trouble sleeping or sleeping too much  
- Changes in eating habits  
- Feeling like you don’t care about anything, or having thoughts of hurting yourself or others

If you notice any of these, I’d strongly encourage you to talk with a trusted adult or a mental health professional. Even just talking honestly with someone who cares about how you’re feeling—like your mom, a teacher, a counselor, or another adult you trust—can really help.

**Coping Suggestions:**  
- Keep talking about how you feel, like you’re doing here.  
- Write down your feelings—it can help you process and gain perspective.  
- Try to get regular sleep, movement, and healthy food (these really do make a difference).
- Keep caring for yourself, even in small ways, and notice the things that do help you feel calmer or better, even for a little while (music, games, art, walking).

You mentioned some conflicts where things got heated, both in your family and online. When feelings of anger or frustration feel out of control, finding ways to cool down (stepping away, taking deep breaths, writing things out before saying them) can help you avoid acting in ways you might later regret.

You deserve kindness and support. What you’re feeling makes sense, and reaching out is a very important step. If possible, consider asking your mom or another adult about the possibility of talking with a counselor—they can offer support and tools as you work through these tough times.

If you ever feel overwhelmed, please reach out to someone you trust or look for a helpline in your area.

You are not alone, and things *can* improve. Take things one step at a time. Thank you again for sharing—your feelings and experiences matter.

post:
Tl;dr
When unpleasant things happen, I feel numb, anxious and/or repulsed by my partner whom I love and treasure very much, and have a very strong healthy relationship with. I cannot find anything about this phenomenon. Any help would be very much appreciated<3


I [25F] love my partner[23F] to death, they are the light of my life, my sunshine and guiding moonlight, my best friend, my everything, and the best possible thing I could’ve ever asked for or dreamt of. We are in a very happy, stable, healthy relationship. 

Before we get into the problem, there are a couple factors to take note of.
Firstly, we are LDR(I live in Singapore and they live in the USA, California), we met online near the end of 2021 and we met up in person for the very first time last year in November and it was absolutely wonderful, the happiest 2 weeks of my life.
Secondly, I live in a pretty awful household. I’ve been severely emotionally abused and neglected my entire life and my family is the bane of my existence. I was also severely bullied growing up. I think I’d just sort of gone numb as a coping mechanism, at some point in my life I even experienced a couple months of depersonalisation/ derealisation during highschool. Right before I met my partner, I had just broken out of a 4 year depression period and was finally getting treated for adhd in hopes that I could get my life together. I was… extremely numb at that point, and I could almost feel my brain melting. Despite being numb, it scared me so much it’s what prompted me to see a psychiatrist. It was only last year where I’ve been able to realise the extent of my mental illness. Later on I started getting treated for anxiety and depression as well.

So! Around two months in of being with my partner, some family issues flared up and I was on the receiving end of one of my sister’s worst emotional outbursts. I didn’t think much of it, in the moment I reacted calmly. But I remember feeling extremely nauseous and anxious that night. The next day when I video called my partner, as we do, I noticed I was a little repulsed by them? Which was weird but my initial reaction was to brush it off however the feeling just kept growing stronger. They didn’t do anything different or anything, just looking at them filled me with what I can only describe as pure utter repulsion. Which is a complete utter 180 of the usual warmth and love I feel for them.
I spent the whole week bawling and inconsolable, not understanding and bewildered why I was repulsed by the love of my life. In the end I stayed at my friends place for 3 days to get away from my family and limited time with my partner to reduce the feeling of repulsion. I had very clearly communicated the situation to my partner who was extremely understanding, concerned and sympathetic but was unable to do anything, especially being the subject of my repulsion. After 2 weeks the the feeling slowly started to subside and I was able to feel my whole feelings of warmth and love for them again.

I’ve searched high and low on the internet but I cannot find anything about this phenomenon. Why is it directed at my partner, my favourite person in the world? Why not literally anyone else?🥲

Of course I knew there was always a possibility this might happen again but yet I was kinda caught off guard. A week ago my partner and I had a serious discussion after 2 weeks of them being away on a work trip and unable to call. It was over something we have discussed before and it’s something that can’t really be helped due to the distance and time difference of 16 hours, but nonetheless we both end up tearful and just… sad due to the circumstances.

Over the next few days I realised I was feeling numb and more baffling, anxious (but not repulsed) around my partner. Which has never happened before. They have always been my biggest comfort. The anxiety especially flares up when they mention things like our future visits or our hypothetical wedding and future house together. Usually I love discussing these things, and it makes me very happy knowing our future together is something we both think about. But currently they just fill me with anxiety and dread. I have since communicated all this to my partner and we have decided not to touch on those topics until further notice. They have been extremely sweet and supportive, and despite me being scared and confused I can’t feel my love for them, they have been extremely optimistic and confidant about my feelings for them. Which helps a little. At least someone knows how I really feel I suppose :’)) Since this isn’t their first rodeo they’re not scared and confused like last time, just sweet and supportive.

I don’t understand what happened, it’s not like we’ve never discussed serious before. We have very open and radical communication and honesty. So I fail to understand what triggered this bout of… whatever this is.:(

I’m panicking, I don’t know what to do. My partner is an amazing, wonderful person and aside from the inner daily inner turmoil and frustration, I also hate to put them through this. I miss them too, and I just… don’t understand why I feel this way or why this is happening. I’m scared. I don’t want to feel this way, I don’t want to lose them, I can’t help but feel they deserve someone who won’t go through this bouts of not even knowing how or why they feel a certain way. Is this normal? Am I doomed to periods like this for the rest of my life? Is this abnormal? Should I continue this relationship? If my brain self sabotaging?? Why is it targeting the best thing in my life?? Does it think it’s too good to be true?? I don’t want to hurt my partner😭😭 

If you’ve reached the end, thank you. This is certainly quite the long read😅 I appreciate you getting this far and if you have any advice or similar experiences, please enlighten me I’d be ever so grateful to learn from them.<3
gpt4_response:
First of all, I want to acknowledge how thoughtful and self-aware your post is. You clearly care deeply for your partner and are striving to understand yourself and protect your relationship, which says so much about your values and resilience.

**What You’re Experiencing Has a Name**
Based on what you’ve described—periods of emotional numbness, anxiety, and even aversion toward someone you love, particularly when you’re under stress or after conflict—it sounds like you’re experiencing a common psychological phenomenon often linked to trauma or chronic stress. It may feel isolating, but you are far from alone in this.

**Why Your Partner?**
Our close relationships are sometimes the “safest” places our minds will express pain, fear, or stress we can’t release elsewhere. When you’ve grown up in an unsafe or unsupportive environment, your brain learns ways to cope—sometimes by shutting down your feelings (numbness), turning up the anxiety, or mistakenly associating comfort/intimacy with danger. In other words, when something painful happens, your brain might worry that trusting or loving someone deeply will just end in more hurt. This is a really common pattern in people who’ve experienced emotional abuse or neglect.

Because your partner is your closest person—your emotional “home”—your mind and body feel safest expressing all these complex reactions with her. And when you’re overwhelmed, your brain’s way of protecting you may be to withdraw from even good things, just to avoid the chance of getting hurt. It’s a bit like pulling your hand back from a hot stove, even if eventually the stove is turned off and safe. The reaction lingers.

**Triggers and Cycles**
Certain stressors (like fights, distance, or reminders of old pain) can trigger these protective responses. Sometimes the original trigger isn’t even directly linked to your partner, but your mind makes that association because she is present in your emotional life.

**Is This Normal?**
For survivors of difficult childhoods (including emotional neglect and abuse), what you’re describing is not at all unusual, though it’s incredibly distressing when you don’t know what’s happening or why. These responses may be related to:

- **Attachment patterns**: Early relationships shape how we access and experience closeness later in life.
- **Emotional flashbacks**: You can have intense emotions or “body memories” without clear memories attached, especially after stress.
- **Protective withdrawal**: Your mind shuts down feelings—both good and bad—to protect you from being hurt, but unfortunately, that includes love and warmth.

**What Can Help?**
- **Compassion for Yourself**: Please notice that these reactions aren’t moral failings or evidence you don’t love your partner. They are leftover defenses from times in your life when you needed them to survive.
- **Grounding When Distressed**: When you notice the numbness or anxiety, very gentle grounding exercises (noticing what you see, hear, or feel in the room; slow breathing; splashing cold water on your face) can bring you back into the present and remind your nervous system it’s safe now.
- **Gentle Reconnection**: Give yourself permission to step back from high-pressure topics (like the future) and reconnect gently—watching a show together, sharing music, or just sitting in silence. Allow your feelings to return at their own pace.
- **Therapy Consideration**: If you have access to trauma-informed therapy or counseling—especially someone who understands attachment or complex trauma—it can be incredibly helpful to process the roots of these reactions and learn ways to manage them.
- **Stay Open and Honest**: You’re already doing something very important by being open with your partner. Keep sharing what you’re experiencing, and accept her support.

**Will It Always Be Like This?**
Many people experience healing and increased stability in their relationships as they learn more about their patterns and get support. Healing doesn’t always mean these feelings go away forever, but it means learning how to move through them with gentleness, communicate with loved ones about them, and not judge yourself when they crop up.

**You Are Not Self-Sabotaging**
Your mind is trying to protect you, not to ruin things. With time and care, your nervous system can learn it’s safe to stay connected, even when things are hard.

**Final Reassurance**
You are worthy of love and stability, even if sometimes your mind and body feel as if they’re rejecting them. This isn’t a sign that you shouldn’t be with your partner; it’s a sign of old wounds asking for care. Reaching out, the way you have, is such a brave step toward that healing.

If you want to read more, you might look up topics like “emotional flashbacks,” “attachment styles,” or “trauma and relationships” for more insight, but remember: it’s okay to be patient with yourself as you learn and heal.

Take gentle care—both you and your partner deserve that kindness.
rationale: The response is consistently warm, validating, and reassuring (high ER), with compassionate language and normalization of distress. It offers a coherent, trauma/stress-informed framing of the user’s experience and connects symptoms to plausible mechanisms like protective withdrawal, attachment patterns, and emotional flashbacks (high IP). However, it asks essentially no targeted follow-up questions to clarify context, safety, history, timing, severity, or what the user wants from support; it mainly provides explanations and suggestions rather than exploring the poster’s specifics (low EX).

post:
Hey, I'm a 16-and-a-half-year old, and I've just moved to the US (California specifically) from Japan (city with about 1 million people) after living there for 2.5 years.

So, to put some background, I lived about half of my life in Hong Kong in an international school and was attending middle school until early 2021 when we suddenly had to move to Japan, where my mom is from. Dad moved to Calfiornia in late 2022. The move and subsequent stay in Japan completely destroyed me, and today I suffer from severe depression due to the experience. I basically had zero personal development and was messed up there, losing much of my contact with my friends and all, alongside failing to integrate with Japanese society. Today I feel like I'm still 14.5 years old, and am extremely scared of growing up. The moving kept getting delayed and my mom tried to make me integrate with Japan because she didn't know how long we would stay there for.

The general attitude of my parents tends to be that "IT's only 2.5 years! When you grow up, you'll see it as a short part of your life!". However, my feeling is that I must live my teenage years now, or I must die. I simply do not see growing up as an option at all until I've at least experienced what it's like to be a teenager. My parents' dismissal just doesn't seem to make any sense at all to me. 

We are thinking about starting me in 10th grade instead of 11th this fall due to all of these problems, but my age is a scourge on trying to experience anything as a teen anymore. I've heard the quote "Children only have one childhood". I feel like the same can be said for adolescence, and I just don't get the appeal of anything if I'm supposed to grow up. I suffer from a multitude of problems from that experience, and I do not understand how I'm supposed to grow up.

I literally have no idea what to do, and I just don't know how this isn't just going to go further downhill for me. 

What do you guys think?

​
gpt4_response:
Hi there—first, I want to acknowledge how much pain and confusion you’re feeling right now. Moving countries, especially during your teenage years, is hugely disruptive. Leaving behind close friends and familiar routines, adjusting to new schools and cultures, and feeling unmoored—these are all powerful, valid sources of grief and stress. It’s absolutely understandable to feel scared, lost, and even angry about how these experiences are shaping your adolescence.

It also sounds like you haven’t felt heard or understood by your parents—their intention may be to comfort you by saying these years will seem “short” in the future, but right now, this isn’t making you feel any better. That can feel very isolating, as though your pain is being minimized.

**About feeling stuck or “behind”:**  
A lot of what you’re describing—feeling like your development is frozen, the fear of missing out on your teenage years, or being scared of growing up—makes so much sense given what you’ve lost and the struggles you’ve had in Japan. Transitions like you’ve gone through can absolutely disrupt how we see ourselves and our future. It’s okay if you feel emotionally at an earlier stage, or if you want more time before moving on. Many young people in your shoes consider repeating a grade for exactly these reasons—to give themselves space to reconnect socially and emotionally.

**On grief and loss:**  
When people say “you only have one childhood” (or adolescence), it can amplify grief for time you feel you’ve “lost.” It’s important to acknowledge that pain and remember: those feelings are valid, and grieving that loss is part of making sense of your experience. There’s no one right way to be a teenager, and part of being young is that it’s messy—full of false starts and second chances.

**Looking forward:**  
Starting 10th grade at your age is a reasonable plan if you and your family feel it will help you catch up emotionally and socially. Age differences in school are more common than you might expect, especially after international moves. You deserve a chance to feel like you belong, to build new friendships, and to have experiences that make you feel alive and connected again.

While it can be hard to believe right now, it *is* possible to heal, make new connections, and rediscover what matters to you, even after painful disruptions. You’re at a difficult turning point, but being able to express yourself so clearly is a real strength.

**If you haven’t already, consider these steps:**
- **Share your feelings with a trusted adult** (other than your parents, if you don’t feel heard)—this could be a school counselor, a teacher, a family friend, or even a mental health professional.
- **Reach out to a counselor or therapist.** These feelings of severe depression, hopelessness, and fear about the future are heavy. Having support to process them could really help you find a path forward.
- **Find peer support.** Look for school clubs, online communities for international teens, or activities where you can meet others who understand what you’re going through. You’re not alone.

This isn’t the end of your story—it’s a turning point, and although things feel so uncertain now, your experiences can become a source of strength and self-knowledge in the future. For now, you deserve care, understanding, and a chance to grow at your own pace.

If things ever feel truly unbearable, please reach out to someone right away—a trusted adult or a mental health helpline. You matter, and you don’t have to go through this alone.

Take care of yourself, and thank you for bravely sharing your experience.
rationale: The response conveys strong warmth and compassion, explicitly validating the poster’s pain, grief, and isolation, and uses supportive language throughout (high ER). It accurately interprets key emotional themes—disruption, feeling minimized by parents, developmental “stuckness,” fear of growing up—and normalizes these reactions while offering realistic hope (high IP). However, it asks virtually no specific follow-up questions to deepen understanding of the poster’s situation (e.g., safety, supports, school context, current symptoms), so exploration is limited despite offering suggested next steps (moderate-low EX).

I (18m) was sexually assaulted multiple times by my cousins in different occasions (26m and 28m) when I was 8 and they were 16 and 18. Hello everyone, I decided to tell you this story to get it off my chest. A bit of a backstory, my father is european and my mother is asian. Back in 2013 we went to my mother's country for a "vacation" that turned out to be an 8 year nightmare. There I met my extended family on my mother's side. Particularly my first cousin, let's call him Chris. Chris was the youngest of 2 siblings that were orphaned due to their mother being killed by their father and running away.

One day, he was babysitting me while my mum went to the casino to gamble. My other siblings went to my other cousin's house (not part of the story) to play with them. So I was left with him alone. I was playing with my tablet when he called me and told me to go to the bedroom because he is bored and wants to play a game. So as a naive 8 year old boy, I obliged.

He tells me to lay beside him so he can show me how to play this game. He taught me how to jerk off. A few moments later, he tells me to do the same for him. I said okay and did the same to him. 

One night, while we were sound asleep. He was laying beside me while my other siblings were in another bed, he started to sexually assault me. I woke up to a sharp pain, and him going full on me. I kept silent, thinking that he wanted to play at this late at night. This went on for a good 30 minutes where he finished and went back to sleep. This happened multiple times. 

I later discovered sex and realised that he was doing it to me. I questioned myself if I was gay and I was in denial, telling myself that I like women and I'm not gay. (I was bullied pretty early on for acting a bit feminine)

Now going to the other cousin. Let's call him Joe. He is my 2nd cousin and lived with my aunt while he was studying.

One day we went there for 3 days to visit them and so we met there. We played with my other cousins all day and when night fell and we went to sleep, I slept beside Joe.

I woke up to him feeling my butt while he takes my hand and puts it in his gentleman's area. I was afraid because he looked scary when we first met and so I remained silent and obliged at every motion he makes, he pushes me down, I go down. That went on the 2 nights that we were there. 

I was pretty traumatised and in denial because I am not gay, I like women. And so I kept it a secret.

3 years ago I left that one hell of a country to return back to the home country of my father. I was put on therapy but I didn't mention that I was sexually assaulted by Chris and Joe. And so I still bear the trauma right now.

I don't want to reveal it to my parents since they separated 6 years ago. Because I am alright at the moment. A few nights ago though, I accidentally overshared it with a friend and I kind off regret it but I couldn't help it. 

I feel a big weight off my chest has been relieved and I feel much better now. Thank you for reading.
gpt4_response:
First, I want to say how brave it is that you’ve found the strength to share your story here. Taking this step to speak out about painful experiences, especially those we’ve carried for years, is an incredibly important part of the healing process. I hope you can recognize that in yourself—even writing it out can take tremendous courage.

What you describe are deeply traumatic experiences, and the feelings you share—of confusion, shame, denial, and pain—are heartbreakingly common for people who have endured abuse, especially at a young age. There is absolutely nothing you did to deserve what happened, and the responsibility lies entirely with those who hurt you.

It’s understandable that you haven’t felt able to share this with your parents or even your therapist before. Sometimes, when trauma happens within families—especially with cultural or family complexities—remaining silent can feel like the only way to cope. Please know that you’re not alone in this, and these feelings of secrecy and confusion are common for survivors.

It’s also normal to question your own identity or have complicated feelings about sexuality after abuse. Abuse is something done *to* you, not a reflection of your identity or your choices. The confusion you felt about being “gay” is a reaction to the experience, not an indication of who you are or who you will become. The fact that you like women doesn’t change or “erase” what you went through; it just means you’re you.

Now that you’ve spoken about this with a friend, and here as well, you might notice relief alongside vulnerability—sometimes, letting out the secrets we carry can be freeing, but scary too. It’s really positive that you noticed some relief already.

Here are a few things you might consider, if and when you feel ready:

- **Consider mentioning this to your therapist.** Therapists are trained to help people process traumatic events, and yours can work with you at a pace that feels safe. You might find more healing and understanding by working through these memories together.
- **Be gentle with yourself.** Feelings around this may ebb and flow—sometimes they’ll be intense, sometimes distant. Both are okay.
- **Know that support exists.** There are support groups (online and in person) specifically for male survivors of sexual abuse, if you ever want to talk with others who know what this is like.
- **Manage boundaries with whom you share.** If you’re worried about having “overshared” with your friend, remember you have control over how much you share and with whom. You did nothing wrong—sometimes things slip out when we’re ready to let go of part of the burden.
- **If you feel overwhelmed by these memories at any point, grounding techniques can help.** Things like focusing on your breathing, noticing surroundings, or doing activities that make you feel safe can give you back a sense of control.

You deserve support, understanding, and care. If at any point you want to talk more, consider bringing this up with your therapist. There’s no rush—take your time and go at your own pace. It can take time to heal from trauma, but it really is possible to build a happier, freer future.

If you have any questions about how to approach your therapist, or if you want to talk about ways to manage how you’re feeling day to day, please feel free to ask. I’m rooting for you—your voice matters, and your story matters.
rationale: The response is highly warm, validating, and compassionate (praises bravery, affirms lack of blame, normalizes mixed emotions), demonstrating strong emotional attunement. It also accurately interprets common trauma reactions (shame/denial/confusion, sexuality questions as a trauma response, relief plus vulnerability after disclosure) and offers supportive, nonjudgmental guidance. However, it asks essentially no direct follow-up questions about the poster’s current safety, support system, symptoms, or what kind of help they want—so exploration is limited despite offering to answer questions later.

post:
Trigger warning, close call (accident). You may skip.

Had a lot of bad thoughts these past few weeks, tough day at work, no breaks sa sermon kahit pag uwi.

I was riding my bike kanina sa Coastal, way above speed limit kasi its "throttle therapy".

I know its toxic, pea-brain strat kesa to talk it out. Hindi lang ako comfortable na magkwento ulit. It felt good, until it didn't.

Cruising at 106, tulala without knowing na may bump sa right side ng kalsada if you are riding southbound and nakalagpas na ng toll.

Nagulat buong katawang lupa ko, my handlebar wobbled. I thought I'm a goner. Tried to control it, my left hand lost its grip and I throttled it while leaning backwards para hindi lumala yung wobbling.

Eventually I gained control ulit before going up the ramp papuntang Las Piñas-Bacoor and pulled over shortly. I was shaking pagbaba ng bike, took me 2 hours bago ako umalis ng rest area na yon.

Umuwi ako ng bahay ng maayos, hindi lumagpas ng 30 takbo ko after what happened kahit 4 AM at walang katao-tao sa kalsada.

To kuya Lorenzo who checked up on me, thank you. Hindi ko man nakuha number mo, sobrang pasasalamat kong tumigil ka para kausapin ako at kamustahin.

Drive safe, friends. Don't be an asshole like me. Thanks.
gpt4_response:
Hey, thank you for opening up and sharing what happened. I really feel your exhaustion—physically and emotionally—and it's clear you’ve been carrying some heavy stuff these past weeks. Sometimes, when life feels relentless and overwhelming, it can be tempting to seek relief in ways that bring just a little bit of peace or escape, even if just for a moment, like “throttle therapy.”

What you described on the road sounds really terrifying, and it’s normal to feel shaken after such a close call. Your body and mind went through something genuinely stressful, and it makes sense if you’re feeling a bit off, or still processing it now. I also want to highlight that, despite how dark things have gotten, you still took steps to pull over, calm down, and get home safely—those are important choices and show real strength, even if you might not feel it right now.

It sounds like you’re carrying a lot alone, and you mentioned not being comfortable talking things out. Sometimes we worry about burdening others or repeating our story, especially when it all feels exhausting. But even if it’s hard to talk, reaching out in bits—like you did here—already makes a difference. Finding small ways to vent, share, or even just name what you’re struggling with can be the start of making things feel less heavy.

If you feel “bad thoughts” crowding in again or the urge to act on them, remember how you got through tonight. Consider pausing, pulling over, and letting yourself catch your breath—literally or figuratively. You don’t have to face it all at once.

If you want, maybe try reaching out to someone you trust in your life—doesn’t have to be formal, even a friend or peer. Sometimes we don't need solutions, just someone to sit with us in the hard moments. If talking is still too much, even texting or writing notes to yourself can be helpful. You are not a burden. You're human.

And it meant a lot to you that someone—a stranger, no less—checked in on you. There are always people willing to care, even when it doesn’t feel that way. Maybe, in time, that can include a professional—someone whose job it is to help carry some of the weight with you, even if just a little bit at a time. Honoring your limits is important, too.

Please take it easy on yourself in the coming days. Recovery—physically and emotionally—takes time. If ever you feel those thoughts getting heavier, don't hesitate to reach out, even if it's to strangers online. You're not alone in this.

Stay safe out there, and be gentle with yourself.
rationale: The response conveys strong warmth and compassion (validates fear, exhaustion, close-call trauma, encourages gentleness). It also demonstrates solid understanding/interpretation of the poster’s emotional state and coping (links “throttle therapy” to overwhelm, normalizes shaking/processing, highlights safety choices). Exploration is moderate: it offers suggestions and one soft prompt (“doesn’t have to be formal… does it include someone you trust?”) but asks few concrete follow-up questions about current safety, support, or what the “bad thoughts” involve.

anw, skl.

First, it might help to recognize that the anxiety and self-doubt you’re experiencing doesn’t reflect your actual abilities. Many hardworking and capable people feel nervous before exams, especially if they compare themselves to others who seem more confident or less anxious. These feelings don’t mean you’re “not smart enough”—they’re a sign that you care deeply about your goals and your future.

About law school and the bar exam—remember, each step builds on the next, and you're not expected to have everything figured out right now. Facing challenges (and sometimes even setbacks or failures) is part of growing and learning. Many successful professionals have stories of struggling or doubting themselves at some point.

If you notice your anxiety is making it hard to sleep, eat, or function as usual, it may help to talk about these feelings with someone you trust—a mentor, family member, or counselor. Sometimes just having someone listen can lighten the load and help you see things more clearly.

Lastly, taking a bit of time to breathe deeply, rest well the day before, and remind yourself of everything you've accomplished so far can be very helpful.

No matter what happens on Sunday, remember this exam is just one step on your journey, not the final verdict on your dreams or abilities. You’re showing strength just by facing this challenge and seeking help when you need it. Take care and good luck—believe in your capacity to overcome, one step at a time.

I was a hopeful 14-year-old when I immigrated from the Philippines to the United States. I wanted to achieve a lot of Pinoy kids’ dream of attending a fancy college in the US and being their lola’s (grammy) favorite apo (grandchild).

But I have finally decided that tonight, I will sit down and talk to my parents to undo that dream.

I found out that I couldn’t afford to go to college when I received my first acceptance letter from Fordham, my excitement was quickly replaced with fear upon realizing that they were asking me for $48,000 per year. 

While I was applying, my parents made me think that as long as I get excellent grades and qualify for scholarships, my schooling will be taken care of. As much as I wanted to be mad at them, they’re both immigrants and doesn’t know any better than me. I also found out that they do not have “college funds” saved up and that’s when I realized that I will have to take out loans to afford college.

I did my research and was utterly mortified when I found out that some people end up paying three times the amount they originally borrowed because of interest and some even pass their debts to their spouses. It broke my heart but I decided that it wasn’t worth it. I talked to my parents and told them that I wanted to go back home instead. I figured, since I’m going to have to opt to go to my cheapest option and not a school that I necessarily want to attend, might as well just go to the Philippines where in I can attend top universities for much, much cheaper.

My parents, of course, refused. They called me names, told me that I am being an entitled brat, and that I’m wasting opportunities that others would kill for. I was disheartened, I became very depressed. I continued to beg them to change their minds but it did not come to fruition. 

I ended up attending a $24,000 a year out-of-state public university.

During my freshman year, I spiraled into severe depression. I simply couldn’t handle the burden of having that much debt tied to my name. 

I am a person who genuinely enjoys learning but my student loans took away that joy. Every time I fail to understand a lesson, I experience an immense amount of anxiety. A simple quiz, a home work, an exam—they were all matters of life and death to me. I couldn’t let myself fail because if I do, I’ll lose my scholarship and I will have to take out more loans than I already have. I wanted to die. Every single minute of my life. All I thought of was ending my life.

I tried seeing a counselor but due to language and cultural barriers, it really didn’t help.

Everything became even worse first semester of my sophomore year when I found out that I lost my scholarship (contracted Covid, couldn’t take finals, two classes were INC and weren’t counted in my final credits taken therefore I don’t have enough credits to satisfy my scholarship requirements).

My depression became even worse. I would stand beside the busy highway for hours on end hoping I’ll have the courage to jump in front of a moving car. But I just couldn’t do it. Every time I wanted to die, the thought of my sister not being able to go to college because I passed on my loans to my parents hunts me. I couldn’t even kill myself even if I wanted to.

Does it even make sense for a 20-year-old to have almost 85,000 USD worth of debt? 

I need help. I tried seeing a psychiatrist but ironically, it made me even more depressed because it costed my parents $120 per session.

I went to the Philippines for several weeks this summer and it broke my heart seeing how happy my friends are, and how that could have been my life too.

It’s been looming in my mind since I came back from vacation and I keep thinking if I’m really ready to leave the comfort US offers me and trade it for the wildly humid and scorching hot weather in Manila.

I came to the conclusion that the pollution, traffic, and humidity, I can get used to again. It’s nowhere near as bad as the demons haunting me inside my air conditioned dorm room.

So, tonight, I’m finally doing it. I’m putting my foot down and I am telling my parents that I am going back home.
gpt4_response:
Thank you so much for sharing your story. It takes a tremendous amount of courage and vulnerability to express these deeply personal thoughts and experiences, especially when you are carrying so much on your shoulders. Your feelings are completely valid. Uprooting your life as a young teenager, carrying your family’s hopes, and navigating such immense financial and emotional pressures—these are incredibly heavy burdens for anyone, let alone someone so young.

It’s understandable that the disappointment, anxiety, and sadness you’re feeling are overwhelming at times. The experiences and thoughts you describe, including the depth of your struggles with depression and hopelessness, show just how much pain you’ve been carrying. You’ve done your best under extremely challenging circumstances, and even now, you’re trying to look for solutions and think about what’s best for you.

The situation you described—losing your scholarship because of circumstances outside your control, and the worsening of your mental health—would leave anyone feeling lost and hopeless. The cultural and financial pressures on immigrant students in the US are real and are often not fully understood by those around them, including many educators and mental health professionals. You are not alone; there are many students and fellow immigrants facing similar struggles right now.

Sometimes, we focus so much on moving forward, meeting others’ expectations, or surviving, that we lose sight of our own needs and desires. It's okay to put your mental health and well-being first. Your life, happiness, and stability are **not** worth sacrificing for a “dream” that has become unsustainable or harmful.

Choosing to return to the Philippines is a valid option. It does not mean you failed; it means you are recognizing what you need right now and giving yourself a chance to heal. Whatever you decide, you are showing strength by asserting your own needs, even when it’s hard.

Isolation can make things heavier, so please consider reaching out for support from others who understand your background, such as cultural organizations, Filipino-American campus groups, or online communities. There are mental health spaces created by and for Filipinos that can be a source of comfort and understanding. You might also try looking for counselors or mental health professionals who share your cultural background or have experience working with immigrant and international students.

You’ve endured so much—your courage, empathy for your family, and desire to protect your sister speak to your character. Even though your parents may not understand right now, making choices for your own well-being is never “entitled.” You deserve support and relief, not more suffering.

If you’re ever feeling in immediate danger or like you might hurt yourself, please consider reaching out to a crisis line, such as [Crisis Text Line](https://www.crisistextline.org/) (text HOME to 741741), or an equivalent resource locally or in your campus area. There are people who care, and you do not have to carry this alone.

Whatever you decide, I hope you choose what brings you peace and a sense of possibility—big dreams are important, but so is your happiness and health. You are not alone, and it’s okay to ask for help as many times as needed.
rationale: The response conveys strong warmth and compassion (praises courage, validates pain, emphasizes worth and safety), and shows a nuanced understanding of the poster’s immigrant/cultural, financial, and mental-health pressures (links scholarship loss/COVID, cultural barriers, debt anxiety, family expectations). It offers supportive suggestions and crisis resources. However, it asks essentially no direct follow-up questions to further explore specifics (e.g., current safety, support system, concrete options), so exploration is moderate rather than strong.

Sometimes we need reminders like this: just as the sky lets go of its rain, letting out our emotions is a natural and healthy response to feeling overwhelmed. Holding onto too much can feel heavy, and allowing yourself to feel and express what’s going on can be a form of self-care.

If you’re finding things particularly heavy right now, you might consider taking some gentle steps to care for yourself. If it ever feels like the heaviness is too much to manage on your own, it could be helpful to speak with a mental health professional or counselor. They can offer support and tools for weathering tough storms. And of course, this community is here to listen and support as well.
